title: The Teacher as Researcher: Action Research for Classroom Improvement
aim: Transform educators from passive curriculum users into reflective, data-informed classroom designers who use short action-research cycles to improve learning outcomes.
overview: This module introduces teachers to a practical action-research method that begins with one observable classroom problem, collects simple baseline evidence, tests one intervention for five days, and reviews the result without labelling students negatively.
learning_objectives: Define a classroom problem in objective, observable terms.
Collect baseline evidence that can be tracked in less than three minutes per day.
Design a five-day intervention that changes one variable in the learning environment.
Compare pre- and post-intervention evidence to decide whether to continue, adapt, or replace the strategy.
Use action-research logs to discuss classroom improvement with colleagues and school leaders.
outcomes: Participants produce a completed Action Research Proposal Canvas.
Participants can distinguish a complaint from a researchable classroom question.
Participants can identify one student-support need without using deficit labels.
Participants can use evidence to justify a practical classroom change.

session_flow.segment: Core exposition video and trainer framing
session_flow.activity: Identity shift from curriculum consumer to action researcher; action-research cycle diagram; example of variable isolation.
Analyse a Grade 4 reading-comprehension scenario where students struggle with informational texts.
Complete the Action Research Proposal Canvas for a real classroom issue.
Quiz and three-sentence application reflection.

trainer_script: Welcome, colleagues. In the Finnish Paradigm, teacher quality is not driven only by external inspection or compliance paperwork. It is strengthened when teachers observe learning carefully, identify barriers, test focused changes, and reflect on evidence. Today, we shift from asking, “What is wrong with this student?” to asking, “What is happening in the learning design, and what can I adjust?” A research-informed teacher does not need a university laboratory. The classroom is the laboratory. The evidence can be simple: exit slips, error counts, participation marks, completion rates, confidence checks, or observed behaviour patterns. The key is to isolate one variable and improve it deliberately.
scenario: A Grade 4 teacher notices that students perform well with narrative texts but struggle with informational science texts. Instead of calling the class lazy or unfocused, the teacher observes that students cannot locate key ideas in dense paragraphs. The teacher introduces a text-mapping anchor card for five days and tracks exit-slip accuracy before and after the intervention.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: Before we read, show me where the important idea might be hiding on this page.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Student
demonstration_dialogue.line: I don’t know. It looks like one big block of words.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: Good observation. Today we will use the text-map card to find headings, signal words and key facts before reading every sentence.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher reflection
demonstration_dialogue.line: The issue may be structural navigation, not attention. I will track whether the visual map improves exit-slip accuracy.

activities.name: Classroom Action Research Proposal Canvas
steps: Choose one academic or behavioural issue.
Rewrite the issue in observable language.
Select one baseline metric for three days.
Choose one intervention for five days.
Write one backup strategy if improvement is below target.

activities.name: Complaint-to-Question Conversion
steps: Convert it into a measurable classroom question.
Share with a partner for clarity review.

quiz_items: Which statement is an observable classroom problem?
Why should an intervention change only one variable at a time?
What evidence can be collected in under three minutes per day?
assessment.performance_task: Submit an action-research canvas for one current classroom issue.
assessment.completion_criteria: Canvas includes one observable deficit, baseline metric, five-day intervention, post-data metric and iteration plan.

admin_implementation: Replace some compliance-only lesson-plan checks with monthly action-research reviews.
Provide 45 minutes per week or fortnight for teacher discussion of intervention logs.
Use evidence from action-research logs to guide resource allocation.

title: Multi-Tiered Support Systems: General, Intensified and Special Support
aim: Equip educators to identify learning needs early and apply structured support through a flexible three-tier model adapted for different school contexts.
overview: This module adapts Finland’s three-level support logic into a practical school model: classroom support for all learners, targeted short-term support for persistent gaps, and individualized planning for learners requiring intensive support.
learning_objectives: Explain the difference between general, intensified and special support.
Use classroom evidence to decide when a learner needs additional support.
Design Tier 1 and Tier 2 adaptations that can be applied without stigma.
Create a support matrix for one learner or small group.
Escalate concerns professionally using evidence and review cycles.
outcomes: Participants complete a Three-Tier Adaptation Matrix.
Participants can identify appropriate support actions for common classroom scenarios.
Participants can avoid confusing language acquisition difficulty with special educational need.
Participants can document support before referral.

session_flow.activity: Three-tier support explanation, examples, and warning signs.
Analyse a Grade 3 fractions case with flexible in-class targeted support.
Build a Three-Tier Classroom Adaptation Matrix.
Scenario quiz and support decision reflection.

trainer_script: Educational support should not begin after repeated failure. In the Finnish Paradigm, support starts early, remains dignified, and becomes more structured only when evidence shows that ordinary classroom support is not enough. Tier 1 is for all students: clear instructions, visuals, modelling, differentiation and feedback. Tier 2 is targeted: a short, planned support cycle for students with repeated difficulty. Tier 3 is intensive: individualized planning when the learner needs sustained adjustments. In schools where co-teaching is possible, teachers may collaborate directly. Where it is not possible, the same support logic can operate through support pods, retired teacher mentors, learning assistants or scheduled intervention windows. The principle is flexible support, not forced co-teaching.
scenario: During a Grade 3 fractions lesson, three learners repeatedly confuse part-whole relationships. The teacher places them at a support pod during independent practice, uses pattern blocks and a visual fraction mat, and records daily success out of five tasks.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: Show me how many triangles cover the whole hexagon.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Student
demonstration_dialogue.line: Six triangles cover it.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: Good. If one triangle is removed, what fraction of the whole is missing? Use the blocks before writing.

activities.name: Three-Tier Classroom Adaptation Matrix
steps: Design a four-week Tier 2 support cycle.
Define quantitative and qualitative progress measures.
Write the condition that would trigger Tier 3 review.

activities.name: Support Decision Sort
steps: Sort each into Tier 1, Tier 2 or Tier 3.

resources: Language Acquisition vs SEN Reflection Sheet

quiz_items: What distinguishes Tier 1 from Tier 2 support?
What evidence should be collected before escalation?
Why can language acquisition difficulty be mistaken for special educational need?
assessment.performance_task: Create a Tier 2 support plan for a learner with repeated difficulty.
assessment.completion_criteria: Plan includes focus skill, frequency, materials, progress measure, review date and next-step trigger.

admin_implementation: Create a monthly student-support review cycle.
Provide a protected time block for teacher/support staff coordination.
Require documented Tier 2 evidence before formal referral, while allowing urgent safeguarding exceptions.
Provide flexible options: special educator, learning assistant, retired mentor, peer support or intervention club.

title: The Pedagogy of Play: Guided Play and Discovery Zones
aim: Help early childhood educators transform desk-based activities into structured, playful learning environments that develop language, numeracy, executive function and social skills.
overview: This module positions play as the core learning method for young children. Teachers learn how to design discovery zones, use open-ended materials and scaffold thinking without taking control away from children.
learning_objectives: Explain the difference between free play, guided play and teacher-directed tasks.
Design discovery zones aligned to early literacy, numeracy and social development.
Use open-ended prompts to support child reasoning during play.
Convert a worksheet task into a movement-based play activity.
Observe executive function behaviours during play.
outcomes: Participants create a Play-Scaffold Conversion Blueprint.
Participants can design one discovery zone with materials and prompts.
Participants can explain play-based learning to parents or administrators.
Participants can document learning without relying on worksheets.

session_flow.activity: Guided play, discovery zones and open-ended prompt modelling.
Analyse a construction-play scenario where learners explore shapes and stability.
Convert a worksheet into a discovery-zone activity.
Prompt quality reflection and quick quiz.

trainer_script: Play is not a break from learning. In early childhood, play is a major route through which children develop language, problem-solving, self-regulation and social understanding. The teacher’s role is not to control every move. The teacher designs a rich environment, observes carefully and uses questions that extend thinking. A worksheet asks children to identify a triangle. A discovery zone lets children test which shape makes a stable roof, explain why a round block rolls and compare objects through action.
scenario: Children build a shelter with cardboard and wooden blocks. The roof keeps falling because a round log is used as the base. The teacher kneels at eye level and asks children what they notice about the shape rather than fixing the structure.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: I notice your roof is sliding. What do you notice about the block underneath?

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Child
demonstration_dialogue.line: It rolls away.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: Which shape could make the base wider and flatter?

activities.name: Prompt Rewrite Practice
steps: Rewrite them as open-ended scaffolding questions.
Role-play the prompts with a partner.

resources: Parent Explanation Handout: Why Play Is Learning

quiz_items: Which prompt best supports guided play?
What is the difference between a discovery zone and a reward play area?
Which observation shows executive function?
assessment.performance_task: Convert one worksheet into a discovery-zone activity.
assessment.completion_criteria: Activity uses movement, open-ended materials, no worksheet dependency and at least three scaffolding prompts.

admin_implementation: Audit KG classrooms for movement space and flexible materials.
Evaluate teachers through observation of scaffolding language, not worksheet completion.
Communicate the academic value of play to parents.

title: Formative Documentation and Joy of Learning
aim: Train educators to document young children’s learning through photos, child voice, teacher notes and growth conversations instead of comparative grading.
overview: Teachers learn how to capture meaningful learning evidence and link it to transversal skills, self-regulation, collaboration and problem-solving.
learning_objectives: Define pedagogical documentation in early learning contexts.
Capture visual, verbal and observational evidence efficiently.
Write narrative comments that focus on process rather than ranking.
Link observed behaviour to development and transversal skills.
Use documentation in parent-child-teacher growth conversations.
outcomes: Participants complete one narrative documentation entry.
Participants can collect evidence in under one minute during classroom activity.
Participants can use child dictation as assessment evidence.
Participants can explain why progress documentation supports joy of learning.

session_flow.activity: Pedagogical documentation, child voice and process evidence.
Analyse a child problem-solving sequence with photos and dictation.
Write a narrative documentation profile.
Quality-check one documentation entry.

trainer_script: Young children should not experience learning as comparison. Formative documentation helps teachers, children and parents see growth over time. A photograph alone is not enough. A strong documentation entry includes the learning sequence, the child’s own words and the teacher’s interpretation of the skill being developed. This preserves joy while giving educators meaningful evidence.
scenario: A child tries to connect two plastic containers with tape. The tape fails. The child pauses, chooses string and ties the containers. The teacher photographs the sequence and records the child’s explanation.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: Tell me how you solved the problem.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Child
demonstration_dialogue.line: The tape was slippery, so I used string because it wraps tight.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher note
demonstration_dialogue.line: The child demonstrated flexible thinking and problem-solving by changing materials independently.

activities.name: Narrative Documentation Profile
steps: Record or write the child’s words.
Link the evidence to one developmental or transversal skill.

quiz_items: What makes documentation pedagogical rather than administrative?
Why should child voice be included?
Which teacher comment focuses on process rather than product?
assessment.performance_task: Create a portfolio entry using a sample child observation.
assessment.completion_criteria: Entry includes visual sequence, child voice, teacher analysis and skill link.

admin_implementation: Select a secure portfolio platform.
Create guidelines for photos, consent and child privacy.
Replace under-age-7 comparative grades with growth conversations where appropriate and legally permitted.

title: Transversal Competences and the 45/15 Cognitive Rhythm
aim: Help primary schools integrate transversal competences while structuring learning blocks to protect focus, movement and recovery.
overview: Participants redesign a primary schedule into 45-minute learning blocks and 15-minute reset breaks, while mapping subject learning to transversal competencies.
learning_objectives: Explain how movement breaks can support attention and classroom climate.
Map one academic task to a transversal competence.
Design a 45/15 lesson block that includes teaching, practice and transition routines.
Plan low-friction transitions that protect learning time.
Create a schedule adaptation that fits local school constraints.
outcomes: Participants create a 45/15 timetable adaptation.
Participants identify one transversal competence in a standard subject lesson.
Participants plan transition routines for outdoor or indoor movement breaks.
Participants can explain the difference between a break as reward and a break as learning support.

session_flow.activity: Transversal competences, movement, rhythm and attention.
Review a primary schedule and identify focus-fatigue points.
Build a 45/15 schedule and competence map.
Transition-risk reflection.

trainer_script: The Finnish Paradigm does not treat breaks as wasted time. A well-designed learning rhythm protects attention, behaviour and well-being. The 45/15 rhythm can be adapted: in some schools it will be outdoor play; in others it may be structured movement, courtyard circulation, quiet reset stations or supervised physical activity. The goal is to avoid long blocks of passive seat time and to build transversal competences through real activities.
scenario: A Grade 4 teacher ends a mathematics focus block at 08:45, uses a clear two-minute transition routine, sends students to a supervised movement reset, then begins an English block at 09:00 with renewed attention.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: That is our focus mark. Close notebooks, stand, collect jackets and move to the reset route.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher after break
demonstration_dialogue.line: Welcome back. What strategy helped you solve the fraction chart before the reset?

activities.name: 45/15 Timetable and Competence Mapping Lab
steps: Select a three-hour section of your current schedule.
Break it into focus and reset blocks.
Map each focus block to one transversal competence.
Plan one fallback indoor reset option.

quiz_items: What is the purpose of the reset block?
How can a mathematics lesson develop multiliteracy?
What makes a transition routine low-friction?
assessment.performance_task: Submit one adapted morning schedule with competence mapping.
assessment.completion_criteria: Schedule includes realistic timing, subject focus, reset plan and competence link.

admin_implementation: Review bell schedules and supervision duties.
Provide outdoor or indoor movement spaces.
Avoid using recess removal as a default punishment where it undermines student regulation.

title: Student Autonomy and Formative Self-Assessment
aim: Train teachers to build student independence through weekly learning contracts, choice pathways and descriptive self-assessment.
overview: Participants learn to shift students from passive compliance to guided autonomy by using clear learning goals, task choices, self-correction and reflection routines.
learning_objectives: Design a weekly learning contract with multiple routes to the same objective.
Write descriptive self-assessment indicators suitable for primary learners.
Use student self-assessment to plan targeted support.
Create a classroom routine for self-correction and peer support.
Balance autonomy with clear expectations.
outcomes: Participants create one student learning contract.
Participants design a red/yellow/green descriptive rubric.
Participants can run a self-assessment conference.
Participants can use contract evidence to group students for support.

session_flow.activity: Autonomy, learning contracts and self-regulation.
Analyse a Grade 5 perimeter learning contract routine.
Build a learner autonomy contract.
Reflection on releasing control responsibly.

trainer_script: Autonomy does not mean leaving students alone. It means giving students a clear goal, structured choices, visible success criteria and a routine for checking their own progress. A good learning contract lets students choose how they reach the same outcome, while the teacher remains the coach who monitors evidence and intervenes when needed.
scenario: Students work toward mastering perimeter. One student chooses a physical puzzle route, another chooses platform practice, and another asks a peer for help after self-checking two errors.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: Which route will help you master perimeter today?

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Student
demonstration_dialogue.line: I missed composite shapes, so I’ll work with the puzzle first and then check with the key.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: Good. Add that to your mid-week check-in plan.

activities.name: Learner Autonomy Contract Matrix
steps: Create two or three routes to mastery.
Add independent, peer and hands-on options.

quiz_items: What is the difference between choice and lack of structure?
Why should rubrics use descriptive language?
How can self-assessment inform Tier 2 grouping?
assessment.completion_criteria: Contract has one goal, at least two pathways, self-checks and descriptive rubric indicators.

admin_implementation: Avoid public ranking systems that undermine personal progress.
Evaluate lesson plans for student choice, reflection and self-assessment routines.
Support classrooms with accessible self-correction materials.

title: Phenomenon-Based Learning Design
aim: Help teachers design interdisciplinary inquiry modules anchored in real-world phenomena and aligned to subject outcomes.
overview: Participants plan a rigorous phenomenon-based learning unit that connects multiple subjects, student research questions, transversal competences and an authentic final product.
learning_objectives: Differentiate genuine phenomenon-based learning from superficial theme teaching.
Select a real-world anchor phenomenon appropriate for lower secondary learners.
Map at least two subject standards to one shared inquiry project.
Design student-generated research questions.
Create an authentic public product and assessment plan.
outcomes: Participants create a PhenoBL Inquiry Canvas.
Participants can align subject standards to a shared phenomenon.
Participants can define individual accountability in collaborative projects.
Participants can design a final product that requires interdisciplinary thinking.

session_flow.activity: PhenoBL phases, examples and curriculum mapping.
Analyse a Global Food Supply Chains project.
Complete a PhenoBL Inquiry Canvas.
Assessment validity reflection.

trainer_script: Real-world problems are not divided neatly into school subjects. Phenomenon-based learning helps students investigate complex topics through several lenses. The work must remain academically rigorous: every subject involved needs clear outcomes, evidence and assessment criteria. The anchor phenomenon creates purpose; the subject standards provide structure.
scenario: An 8th-grade team studies Global Food Supply Chains. Geography examines routes and supply risk; chemistry examines preservation and decomposition; economics examines pricing and availability. Students create a local food-system risk dashboard.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Student
demonstration_dialogue.line: The fresh berries disappeared after the port delay, but canned fruit stayed available.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Geography Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: What does the logistics map show about transit vulnerability?

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Science Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: What chemical or biological processes explain the difference in shelf life?

activities.name: Cross-Subject PhenoBL Unit Planner
steps: Map two or more subject areas.
Write one research question per subject.
Plan individual and group assessment evidence.

quiz_items: What makes PhenoBL different from theme teaching?
Why must each subject keep clear assessment evidence?
What is an authentic final product?
assessment.completion_criteria: Canvas includes phenomenon, subject standards, research questions, transversal competence and final product.

admin_implementation: Provide shared planning time for subject teams.
Schedule project blocks or flexible timetable windows.
Create school guidelines for assessment, fieldwork and public presentation.

title: Subject-Specific Didactics in High-Trust Environments
aim: Help subject teachers reduce passive lecture time and design high-trust discovery tasks that shift cognitive load to students.
overview: Participants transform a teacher-heavy lesson into a structured inquiry block using an anchor challenge, student investigation and peer synthesis.
learning_objectives: Identify excessive teacher talk-time in lesson plans.
Rewrite a lecture into an anchor challenge and guided inquiry task.
Use open-ended questions to address misconceptions.
Build accountability into independent or group work.
Create a lesson flow that prioritizes student cognitive work.
outcomes: Participants complete a Cognitive Load Pivot Framework.
Participants can limit direct presentation to a focused introduction.
Participants can write guiding questions rather than answer-giving prompts.
Participants can design evidence of student thinking.

session_flow.activity: High-trust didactics, talk-time and cognitive load.
Analyse a thermodynamics lesson where students infer patterns from data.
Redesign a lecture lesson into an inquiry block.
Talk-time audit and confidence reflection.

trainer_script: In many classrooms, the teacher does the hardest thinking while students copy. High-trust didactics require the teacher to design the challenge, provide resources and then step back enough for students to reason. This does not reduce academic rigour. It increases it, because students must explain, test, revise and defend their thinking.
scenario: A physics teacher gives students raw heat-transfer data and asks them to infer why aluminium and glass show different heat-loss rates. The teacher responds to questions with prompts instead of giving the formula first.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Student
demonstration_dialogue.line: The data for aluminium and glass do not match.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: What physical difference do you feel when touching metal compared with glass?

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Student
demonstration_dialogue.line: Metal pulls heat away faster.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: Now find where that material difference appears in your data.

activities.name: Didactic Structural Makeover
steps: Reduce teacher input to a focused anchor challenge.
Plan peer synthesis and accountability evidence.

quiz_items: What is cognitive load in classroom design?
Which teacher prompt supports discovery?
How can a teacher maintain accountability during independent inquiry?
assessment.completion_criteria: Plan limits direct input, includes inquiry task, guiding questions and student evidence.

admin_implementation: Use observation tools that track talk-time and student active work.
Coach teachers to redesign lectures gradually rather than demand instant transformation.
Reward evidence of student reasoning, not just quiet compliance.

title: Modular Upper Secondary Pathways and Individual Study Planning
aim: Support upper secondary educators in designing modular study pathways, advising systems and student accountability structures.
overview: Participants convert a year-long subject into a set of modular course blocks and build a student-facing academic pathway dashboard.
learning_objectives: Explain the purpose of modular study pathways.
Break a year-long curriculum into shorter coherent course modules.
Define entry prerequisites and completion milestones.
Design an Individual Study Plan support process.
Create advising triggers for students who fall behind.
outcomes: Participants produce a Modular Study Course Architecture Sheet.
Participants can write module prerequisites and milestones.
Participants can design an advising workflow.
Participants can identify support needed for students with weak self-direction.

session_flow.activity: Modular systems, individual study planning and accountability.
Review a flexible pathway case for a science-focused student.
Convert one course into a modular pathway.
Advising readiness reflection.

trainer_script: Older students need structured independence. A modular pathway does not simply let students choose randomly. It uses clear course blocks, prerequisites, milestones and advisor check-ins to prepare learners for university and professional responsibility. The school must provide guidance while gradually increasing student ownership.
scenario: A Grade 11 student takes Advanced Organic Chemistry alongside a lighter writing elective to balance workload. An advisor checks prerequisites, milestone planning and support options.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Advisor
demonstration_dialogue.line: You have selected CHEM-03. Have you completed CHEM-01 and CHEM-02?

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Student
demonstration_dialogue.line: Yes, and I scheduled a peer study lab for the week-four review.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Advisor
demonstration_dialogue.line: Good. Add that support plan to your Individual Study Plan.

activities.name: Modular Academic Pathway Dashboard
steps: Divide it into coherent 6–8 week modules.
Add advising triggers and support options.

quiz_items: What makes a modular course coherent?
Why are prerequisites important?
What advising trigger should activate support?
assessment.completion_criteria: Plan includes module title, duration, prerequisites, milestones, assessment evidence and support triggers.

admin_implementation: Pilot modular structure with one subject before whole-school conversion.
Train advisors to monitor progress at each module start and midpoint.
Use data dashboards to identify students at risk of falling behind.

title: EdTech De-Gamification: Starfall, SplashLearn and Low-Stress Stations
aim: Help ESL teachers use digital platforms as calm, collaborative learning stations connected to physical language practice.
overview: Participants redesign digital phonics or vocabulary activities so that timers, leaderboards and reward pressure are reduced, while language practice becomes collaborative and tactile.
learning_objectives: Identify competitive or overstimulating elements in EdTech tasks.
Design a three-station ESL rotation: digital, tactile and guided communication.
Create pair-talk prompts for collaborative tablet use.
Connect digital tasks to physical reinforcement activities.
Observe language engagement rather than points or speed.
outcomes: Participants create a Low-Stress Tech Integration Blueprint.
Participants can redesign a digital activity for pair collaboration.
Participants can link phonics/vocabulary software to tactile practice.
Participants can evaluate student engagement through language use.

session_flow.activity: Motivation, anxiety and balanced station design.
Compare high-speed individual gaming with collaborative station rotation.
Redesign an EdTech activity.
Stress-indicator reflection.

trainer_script: Digital tools can support language learning, but they should not replace thoughtful pedagogy. If students focus only on points, speed or flashing rewards, language processing can become shallow. We will use EdTech as one station in a balanced routine: digital discovery, tactile reinforcement and guided communication.
scenario: Two students share one tablet for a short-vowel activity. The teacher removes speed pressure and asks them to pronounce each target word together before tracing it in sand.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Student A
demonstration_dialogue.line: This word is bed. /b/ /e/ /d/.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Student B
demonstration_dialogue.line: Let’s trace it in the sand tray.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: Say the sound as your finger moves. Slow is good if your pronunciation is clear.

activities.name: De-Gamifying the EdTech Lesson Map
steps: List high-stress features to disable or ignore.

quiz_items: What is a risk of overusing leaderboards?
Why pair students at a digital station?
What evidence shows language learning rather than game-chasing?
assessment.performance_task: Submit a redesigned EdTech station plan.
assessment.completion_criteria: Plan includes digital, tactile and guided communication components.

admin_implementation: Audit app settings for public leaderboards and timer pressure.
Provide tactile materials alongside tablets.
Evaluate teachers on integration quality, not app usage minutes alone.

title: Syllabus Deconstruction and Mastery Folders: Cambridge and Pearson Alignment
aim: Help ESL teachers transform linear coursebook units into student-managed mastery folders aligned to grammar, vocabulary and communicative skill targets.
overview: Participants learn to keep Cambridge/Pearson-style rigour while reducing lockstep pacing by using individualized targets, verification tasks and progress folders.
learning_objectives: Break coursebook units into discrete mastery targets.
Create student-friendly grammar and vocabulary milestones.
Design self-verification tasks and evidence records.
Organize a mixed-ability ESL classroom with different learners working on different targets.
Use mastery folders to inform feedback and support.
outcomes: Participants complete a Mastery Tracking Matrix.
Participants can convert one textbook unit into skill targets.
Participants can create a self-verification process.
Participants can use mastery folders as evidence of progress.

session_flow.activity: From pacing to mastery; deconstructing textbook units.
Analyse a B1 unit converted into narrative verb-tense targets.
Build a mastery folder pathway.
Reflection on pacing and learner ownership.

trainer_script: Completing a coursebook page is not the same as mastering language. A strong ESL classroom can preserve the structure of Cambridge or Pearson materials while letting students track specific sub-skills. Mastery folders make progress visible. They show which grammar point, vocabulary set or communicative function the learner can use independently.
scenario: A B1 class is working on past simple and past continuous. Some learners are ready for narrative writing while others still need irregular verb support. Students work through folder pathways and request verification when ready.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Student
demonstration_dialogue.line: I missed three irregular verbs, so I’m not ready for the narrative paragraph yet.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: Good self-assessment. Complete the corrective review and use the verification key before the final task.

activities.name: Cambridge/Pearson Deconstruction Lab
steps: Identify grammar, vocabulary and skill targets.

quiz_items: Why is page completion not enough evidence?
What makes a mastery target student-friendly?
How can self-verification support autonomy?
assessment.performance_task: Convert one ESL unit into mastery targets.
assessment.completion_criteria: Matrix includes target skills, activities, verification evidence and teacher check.

admin_implementation: Balance coursebook pacing with mastery evidence.
Monitor student progress through portfolios, not only unit tests.
Allow differentiated pathways within department schemes of work.

title: Task-Based Sociolinguistic Inquiry: British Council Communicative Goals
aim: Help teachers turn communicative language outcomes into real-world inquiry projects that build fluency, confidence and local relevance.
overview: Participants design a sociolinguistic inquiry project where learners investigate authentic English use in the community and produce a practical communicative output.
learning_objectives: Define a sociolinguistic inquiry project.
Connect language structures to real community tasks.
Create fieldwork safety and preparation steps.
Design templates that reduce speaking anxiety.
Assess communicative competence through descriptive indicators.
outcomes: Participants create a Sociolinguistic Project Planning Sheet.
Participants can align a communicative language target to field data.
Participants can scaffold real-world interaction.
Participants can design an authentic written or spoken output.

session_flow.activity: Task-based learning, field inquiry and communicative purpose.
Analyse an English signage improvement project.
Design a community inquiry project.
Reflection on anxiety and authenticity.

trainer_script: Language becomes meaningful when learners use it to investigate and improve the world around them. A sociolinguistic inquiry project asks students to observe how English functions in real settings: signage, tourism, business, public information, online communication or community services. The teacher provides language scaffolds and safety structures, but the task has a real purpose.
scenario: A B2 class investigates English signs in a transport centre. Students identify unclear modal verbs and write formal recommendations for improved wording.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Student
demonstration_dialogue.line: This sign says passengers should not cross, but for safety it should say must not cross.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: Good. Which modal verb communicates obligation most clearly? Add that to your recommendation letter.

activities.name: Community Sociolinguistic Inquiry Blueprint
steps: Create a fieldwork data collection template.

quiz_items: What makes a task authentic?
Why do students need fieldwork scaffolds?
How can modal verbs be assessed in a real-world product?
assessment.completion_criteria: Plan includes target language, field data, safety steps, scaffold and final output.

admin_implementation: Create fieldwork permission and supervision procedures.
Approve local inquiry zones.
Assess projects through language outcomes and inquiry quality.

title: IELTS Formative Diagnostics and Student-Led Goal Setting
aim: Train IELTS teachers to reduce over-testing and help students use band descriptors and sub-skill diagnostics to set precise improvement goals.
overview: Participants learn to break Writing and Speaking performance into actionable sub-skills using diagnostic logs, error isolation and learner goal-setting routines.
learning_objectives: Translate public band descriptors into student-friendly checklists.
Identify sub-skill weaknesses in IELTS writing and speaking.
Create an error log that tracks repeated language patterns.
Set student goals based on evidence rather than general band scores.
Plan targeted mini-lessons from diagnostic data.
outcomes: Participants create a Student-Led Diagnostic Matrix.
Participants can identify mechanical cohesion, grammar or vocabulary patterns.
Participants can run a goal-setting conference.
Participants can explain why repeated mock tests alone may not improve performance.

session_flow.activity: Band descriptors, sub-skills and diagnostic thinking.
Analyse a Band 6.0-style writing sample for coherence issues.
Build a sub-skill diagnostic matrix.
Goal-setting reflection and quiz.

trainer_script: A mock score tells students where they are, but not always what to change. Formative IELTS preparation helps students identify the exact barrier: task response, paragraph development, lexical precision, grammatical range, pronunciation, fluency or cohesion. The student becomes a partner in diagnosis, not a passive receiver of a score.
scenario: A student writes many essays but remains at the same approximate band because transitions are mechanical. The teacher uses a diagnostic log to identify repeated patterns and teach more integrated cohesion.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Student
demonstration_dialogue.line: I keep getting the same score even though I wrote four essays.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: More essays will not help if the same issue repeats. Let’s isolate one sub-skill: cohesion within paragraphs.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Student
demonstration_dialogue.line: So I need to replace list-like linkers with transitions that connect ideas more naturally.

activities.name: Sub-Skill Diagnostic Matrix
steps: Review a writing or speaking sample.
activities.deliverable: Completed diagnostic matrix and goal plan.

quiz_items: Why is a band score not enough feedback?
What is a sub-skill diagnostic?
How can students use descriptors without becoming discouraged?
assessment.performance_task: Analyse one sample and create a student goal plan.
assessment.completion_criteria: Matrix identifies criterion, error pattern, alternative structure and practice goal.

admin_implementation: Balance mock testing with diagnostic teaching.
Track sub-skill milestones in student portfolios.
Provide teachers with planning time for feedback and diagnostic grouping.

title: Differentiated Productive Skills: Speaking and Writing Scaffolding
aim: Help IELTS teachers manage mixed-band classes through parallel support, core and extension tasks for speaking and writing.
overview: Participants design three-tier tasks for the same IELTS prompt so weaker learners receive explicit frames, core learners practise standard structures and advanced learners extend sophistication.
learning_objectives: Design support, core and extension versions of one IELTS task.
Provide grammatical frames without trapping students in memorized templates.
Challenge advanced learners with lexical, grammatical and argument complexity.
Manage teacher circulation and peer feedback in mixed-band classes.
Move students between tiers based on evidence.
outcomes: Participants create a Three-Tier Productive Prompt Sheet.
Participants can scaffold IELTS Writing Task 2 or Speaking Part 3.
Participants can challenge Band 7+ learners without neglecting Band 5 learners.
Participants can use feedback loops to encourage upward movement.

session_flow.activity: Tiered scaffolding and productive skills.
Analyse a mixed-band classroom writing task.
Build a three-tier prompt matrix.
Reflection on stigma-free differentiation.

trainer_script: A mixed IELTS class should not force every student into the same template at the same time. Differentiation allows all learners to work on the same exam theme at different levels of support. The goal is not to label learners permanently. The goal is to provide the right support today and help students move upward.
scenario: Students respond to a Writing Task 2 prompt on university funding. Tier 1 receives thesis frames, Tier 2 writes a standard balanced argument, and Tier 3 uses inverted conditionals and nuanced economic vocabulary.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: Support group, use the thesis frame first. Core group, focus on paragraph logic. Extension group, integrate one inverted conditional naturally.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Advanced Student
demonstration_dialogue.line: Had governments invested earlier, the funding crisis might be less severe.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: Good. Now make sure the structure supports the argument, not just the grammar target.

activities.name: Three-Tier Productive Prompt Generator
steps: Choose one IELTS speaking or writing prompt.
Plan feedback and movement between tiers.

quiz_items: How can tiering avoid stigma?
What is the difference between support and simplification?
What makes an extension task genuinely advanced?
assessment.completion_criteria: Matrix contains support, core and extension levels for the same prompt with feedback plan.

admin_implementation: Require differentiated productive-skill options in IELTS lesson plans.
Group students flexibly based on recent evidence.
Avoid fixed labels such as weak/strong in student-facing materials.

title: Phenomenon-Based Receptive Skills: IELTS Reading and Listening
aim: Move IELTS receptive-skills preparation beyond repetitive test drills by using authentic phenomena, macro-text mapping and vocabulary systems.
overview: Participants design a reading/listening lesson based on a real-world academic phenomenon and align text sections to IELTS question types.
learning_objectives: Explain the risk of test fatigue in receptive-skills preparation.
Map the logical structure of an academic text.
Align paragraph sections to IELTS question types.
Teach academic vocabulary through context and recurrence.
Design receptive-skills lessons that combine exam skills with content knowledge.
outcomes: Participants create a Macro-Text Mapping Instructional Sequence.
Participants can link text structure to Matching Headings or True/False/Not Given tasks.
Participants can build a vocabulary extraction system.
Participants can reduce overreliance on practice tests.

session_flow.activity: Macro-text mapping, top-down processing and vocabulary acquisition.
Analyse a microplastics academic text structure.
Build a phenomenon-based receptive lesson.
Reflection on speed, structure and vocabulary.

trainer_script: IELTS reading improvement does not come from endless timed papers alone. Students need to understand how academic texts are built: introduction, claim, evidence, contrast, method, implication and conclusion. Once students see structure, they scan faster and answer more accurately. Phenomenon-based reading also improves vocabulary because words are learned in meaningful contexts.
scenario: Students read an article on microplastics. Instead of translating every sentence, they map sections: causes, research methods, data shift, consequences and proposed solutions. They then connect these sections to IELTS question types.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Student
demonstration_dialogue.line: I get stuck reading every word.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Teacher
demonstration_dialogue.line: Before reading every word, identify the paragraph function. Is this cause, method, evidence or conclusion?

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Student
demonstration_dialogue.line: Paragraph B explains the method, so it matches the heading about research design.

quiz_items: What is macro-text mapping?
How does text structure support Matching Headings?
Why should vocabulary be taught through context?
assessment.performance_task: Design one phenomenon-based IELTS reading/listening lesson.
assessment.completion_criteria: Plan includes authentic text/source type, structure map, question alignment and vocabulary system.

admin_implementation: Provide teachers with access to age-appropriate authentic articles.
Balance practice tests with deep reading/listening instruction.
Track vocabulary growth and question-type accuracy.

title: Annual Implementation, Quality Assurance and Administrative Rollout
aim: Enable school leaders to implement the Finnish Paradigm gradually through infrastructure review, staff training, curriculum activation and quality assurance.
overview: This leadership module turns the curriculum into a school-year rollout with practical checkpoints for environment, scheduling, support systems, teacher collaboration, student portfolios and audits.
learning_objectives: Build a phased implementation timeline.
Prioritize infrastructure changes that support pedagogy.
Plan staff training across grade tracks.
Create a quality assurance audit calendar.
Use evidence from action research, support logs and student portfolios to guide improvement.
outcomes: Participants produce a 9-month implementation roadmap.
Participants define leadership responsibilities for each phase.
Participants create audit metrics for teacher talk-time, support logs and mastery evidence.
Participants identify risks and mitigation steps.

session_flow.activity: Implementation phases and change-management principles.
Review a school rollout scenario and identify risks.
Build a school implementation roadmap.
Leadership readiness self-audit.

trainer_script: A school does not become more student-centred because of one workshop. Implementation requires sequence, support and evidence. Leaders must decide what to pilot first, how teachers will be trained, how progress will be monitored and how student dignity will be protected. The Finnish Paradigm is strongest when leaders remove obstacles rather than simply demand new paperwork.
scenario: A school begins with three pilot areas: Grade 1–3 45/15 rhythm, Grade 4–6 learning contracts and Grade 7–9 support matrices. Leaders collect evidence monthly and avoid whole-school overload.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Principal
demonstration_dialogue.line: We will not launch everything at once. Which two grades are ready for a first pilot?

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Academic Director
demonstration_dialogue.line: Primary can begin with learning contracts, while lower secondary pilots support matrices.

demonstration_dialogue.speaker: Principal
demonstration_dialogue.line: Good. Define evidence, support time and review dates before launch.

quiz_items: Why should implementation be phased?
What evidence shows a support system is working?
How can leaders avoid turning innovation into extra paperwork?
assessment.completion_criteria: Roadmap includes phases, owners, resources, QA metrics and risk plan.

admin_implementation: Begin with pilots before full rollout.
Protect teacher planning time.
Audit student outcomes and teacher workload.
Review legal, safeguarding and privacy requirements before storing learner intervention data.
